Preprint — 2025

Entropy-Gradient Grounding: Training-Free Evidence Retrieval in Vision-Language Models

Marcel Gropl*,1,  Jaewoo Jung*,3,  Seungryong Kim3,  Marc Pollefeys1,  Sunghwan Hong1,2

* Equal contribution

1ETH Zurich  •  2ETH AI Center  •  3KAIST

arXiv Paper

Figure 1. Our method improves LLaVA-1.6 on fine-grained spatial reasoning (a) and multi-region visual grounding (b). The spider plot (c) summarises gains across six benchmarks versus LLaVA-1.6 and ViCrop.

Abstract

Despite rapid progress, pretrained vision–language models still struggle when answers depend on tiny visual details or on combining clues spread across multiple regions, as in documents and compositional queries. We address this by framing grounding as test-time evidence retrieval: given a query, the model should actively identify where to look next to resolve ambiguity. To this end, we propose a training-free, model-intrinsic grounding method that uses uncertainty as supervision. Specifically, we compute the entropy of the model’s next-token distribution and backpropagate it to the visual token embeddings to obtain an entropy-gradient relevance map, without auxiliary detectors or attention-map heuristics. We then extract and rank multiple coherent regions to support multi-evidence queries, and introduce an iterative zoom-and-reground procedure with a spatial-entropy stopping rule to avoid over-refinement. Experiments on seven benchmarks across four VLM architectures demonstrate consistent improvements over existing methods, with the largest gains on detail-critical and high-resolution settings, while also producing more interpretable evidence localizations.

Method

Figure 2. Overview of our approach. (a) We compute an entropy-gradient map from the model’s first-token distribution to identify the initial region of interest. (b) An iterative refinement loop updates the crop until spatial entropy stops decreasing. (c) The final forward pass uses the refined crop to produce the answer.

Table 1. Quantitative results on standard reasoning benchmarks. Our training-free method improves fine-grained image understanding across four VLM architectures.

Figure 10. Qualitative results with LLaVA 1.5. Our method correctly localises fine-grained regions, fixing errors in text, appearance, and spatial queries.

Figure 11. Qualitative results with LLaVA 1.6, InternVL-3.5, and Qwen 2.5 VL. Consistent improvements across architectures on fine-grained visual grounding.

Figure 3. Qualitative comparison on a document VQA example. Relative-attention maps focus on the wrong region (answer: 6), while our gradient-based map correctly localises the relevant cell (answer: 696).
